description: Cross-team planning meeting touching POS, mobile, and pharmacy. The system must produce one story per concrete ask AND catch the NS-389 loyalty duplicate.

deterministic_metrics.name: acceptance_criteria_well_formed
observations: 4/4 stories have AC count in range

deterministic_metrics.name: expected_duplicates_found
observations: Expected duplicate (['loyalty', 'tier'] ↔ NS-389) not found at confidence ≥ medium

reasons.ac_quality: Most acceptance criteria are concrete and testable with specific metrics (p95 < 2s, 5% margin, 7-day monitoring), though ST-03's criteria could be more precise about what constitutes acceptable regression.
reasons.priority_justification: Priorities are well-justified with clear business impact (customer complaints, corrupted metrics, trust erosion), though ST-03's 'Medium' could better explain why Q3 timeline doesn't elevate urgency.
reasons.story_granularity: Stories are appropriately sized as single deliverable behaviors (fix receipt latency, fix deep links, evaluate upgrade, eliminate duplicates), though ST-03 might be split into assessment and implementation stories.
reasons.tag_accuracy: Tags are contextually appropriate and drawn from a consistent domain vocabulary (pos, mobile-app, pharmacy, performance, compliance), with guardrails correctly flagging non-canonical additions.
reasons.conflict_reasoning: ST-04 conflicts correctly identify relevant HIPAA constraints (C-08, C-09, C-10), but the reasoning is weak—the acceptance criteria *do* explicitly address opt-in (C-08), verified delivery (C-09), and audit logging (C-10), so these should not be flagged as conflicts.

llm_judge.overall_comment: This is a strong backlog synthesis with well-structured stories, clear traceability to source topics, and comprehensive task breakdowns. The primary weakness is incorrectly flagged conflicts for ST-04, where the acceptance criteria actually do address the cited constraints, suggesting the conflict detection logic needs refinement to recognize when requirements are already incorporated.
